coredata.dc:description: The development of TiO2-based photocatalysts is significantly impeded by the limited photoresponsive and the rapid recombination of charge carriers generated by photons. In hence, a novel photocatalytic system of titanium dioxide nanotubes (TNTs) loaded Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots (TNTs/Ag/CDs) was developed, which quickly transferred charges, absorbed visible light, generated more reactive oxygen species, and demonstrated excellent photocatalytic degradation ability and anti-bacteria properties. Under the synergistic effect of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Ag NPs and high charge transfer efficiency of CDs, photo generated electron and hole recombination was suppressed, and visible light absorption was enhanced. The loading of Ag and CDs was excessive or insufficient to decrease the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst. Within 120 min under visible light, the methylene blue (MB) degradation efficiency of TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst was higher than that of binary photocatalyst or TNTs photocatalyst, demonstrating excellent photocatalytic performance. The sterilization rate against E.coli and S.aureus of the TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst was about twice that of TNTs. Therefore, TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst provides ideas for designing high-performance photocatalysts and exhibits excellent potential in the field of wastewater treatment and other forms of environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Anti-bacteria performance of titanium dioxide nanotubes loaded Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots for photocatalytic under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of TiO2-based photocatalysts is significantly impeded by the limited photoresponsive and the rapid recombination of charge carriers generated by photons. In hence, a novel photocatalytic system of titanium dioxide nanotubes (TNTs) loaded Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots (TNTs/Ag/CDs) was developed, which quickly transferred charges, absorbed visible light, generated more reactive oxygen species, and demonstrated excellent photocatalytic degradation ability and anti-bacteria properties. Under the synergistic effect of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Ag NPs and high charge transfer efficiency of CDs, photo generated electron and hole recombination was suppressed, and visible light absorption was enhanced. The loading of Ag and CDs was excessive or insufficient to decrease the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst. Within 120 min under visible light, the methylene blue (MB) degradation efficiency of TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst was higher than that of binary photocatalyst or TNTs photocatalyst, demonstrating excellent photocatalytic performance. The sterilization rate against E.coli and S.aureus of the TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst was about twice that of TNTs. Therefore, TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst provides ideas for designing high-performance photocatalysts and exhibits excellent potential in the field of wastewater treatment and other forms of environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, it is urgent to purify industrial wastewater containing pollutants, due to the harmful effects of heavy metals and organic dyes on humans and aquatic communities [1]. Addressing this challenge requires effective and sustainable methods for pollutant removal, with photocatalyst emerging as a promising solution [2]. Recent research has focused on various nanostructured photocatalysts to improve the efficiency of purifying industrial wastewater. For instance, Bareke [3] developed a new bimetallic LiCu-ferrite nanospinel, which exhibited bacteriostatic and bactericidal effects against S. aureus and E. coli. Mustafa [4] synthesizes a bimetallic organic frameworks (Mn-Al-MOF) via hydrothermal technique and Mn-Al-MOF achieved 100 % degradation of anionic dye in the presence of 1 mM K2S2O8. Oladipo [5] prepared a CoO-NiFe2O4 mixed metal oxide catalyst, which accomplished 87–97 % decolorization of eriochrome black T. Mustafa [6] prepared an Ag-doped Zn0.5Ni0.5Fe2O4 (FZN) catalyst. It has good degradation efficiency of metronidazole antibiotic and antibacterial ability. Additionally, FZN catalyst, which is easy to recover from solution, was doped with Mg, enhancing its performance in trichlorophenol degradation and microbe inactivation [7].

Among the various photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is widely studied because of its chemical stability, low cost, and environmental friendliness, making it a prime candidate for photocatalytic applications such as water and air purification [8,9]. However, wide bandgap (3.2 eV) of TiO₂ restricts its activation to UV light, which comprises only 3–5 % of sunlight, thus limiting its efficiency under visible light irradiation [10,11]. To address this limitation, TiO₂ nanotubes (TNTs) have been explored due to their larger specific surface area compared to nanoparticles, providing more active sites and reducing electron-hole recombination [12,13]. To overcome visible light limitation of TNTs, various modification strategies have been explored, including metal and non-metal doping, heterojunction formation, and surface modifications. Among these, metals have gained attention because of their surface plasmon resonance (SPR effect), which enhances visible light absorption and reduces electron-hole recombination [14,15]. The photocatalytic activity of TNTs when exposed to visible light can be enhanced by plasmonic metals, which have distinctive SPR characteristics. Plasmonic metals include silver (Ag), gold (Au), and platinum (Pt) [16,17]. Furthermore, these metals also have other advantages, such as the ability to absorb electrons, postpone the recombination of photo-induced electron (e−) - hole (h+) pairs owing to a change in the Fermi level, and increase the number of catalytic sites, all of which extend the catalytic operation. They are able to exchange charge carriers produced by light thanks to this hybrid semiconductor plasmonic nanostructure [18]. In particular, the introducing Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) can significantly enhance performance of TNTs by extending light absorption into the visible range and preventing rapid electron-hole recombination.

The SPR effect of Ag NPs can enhance the absorption of visible light in the wavelength range of 400 to 800 nm [19]. Rufina [20] prepared Ag-TNTs nanotube arrays and the results indicated that under visible light driving, the hydrogen production rate of Ag-TNTs was 12 times that of TNT. Lincho [21] loaded Ag onto the surface of TNTs through photo deposition. The phenol degradation experiment showed that the composite photocatalyst achieved phenol removal rate of 12 % under visible light irradiation. However, binary catalysts exist limitations such as insufficient stability and limited photocatalytic ability. To overcome these bottlenecks, researchers have begun introducing a multi-component to utilize the synergistic effects among multiple components, thereby enhancing the performance of the catalyst. Feng [22] synthesized a stable sodium complex of silver @ titanium carbide @ cuprous oxide with good antibacterial activity. The nanocomposites have remarkable antibacterial efficacy against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus. Sun [23] developed an Ag/AgI@Bi2O2CO3/3DOM TiO2 photocatalyst with a plasmon resonance effect and double Z-scheme heterostructure, achieving Rhodamine B removal rate (99.8 %) under full spectrum irradiation.

In addition to metal-based modifications, carbon dots (CDs) have shown promise in enhancing TiO₂ photocatalyst because of their excellent biocompatibility, wide light absorption range, and electron conduction capabilities [24,25]. Incorporating CDs can further improve electron-hole separation, enhancing visible light activity of TiO₂ [26–28]. Wei et al. prepared Ag NPs/CDs/TNTs composite with enhanced photocatalytic activity via a simple one-step method [29]. The prepared composite photocatalyst exhibited higher photocatalytic activity, which was attributed to the suppression of photo generated electron hole recombination. The photoelectric properties of CDs enhanced the visible light absorption. Li et al. prepared a CDs/Bi: TiO2 photocatalyst, and the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst for phenol and rhodamine was significantly enhanced under simulated sunlight [30]. This catalysts have excellent charge transfer ability and absorb visible light. Therefore, the composite of CDs with Ag and TiO2 can effectively enhance the photocatalytic ability.

This study aims to overcome the limitations of traditional TNTs photocatalysts in terms of photocatalytic performance and stability by synthesizing a novel TNTs loaded Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots ternary composite material (TNTs/Ag/CDs). Composite materials utilize the SPR effect of Ag NPs and the optoelectronic properties of CDs to expand the light response range. Schottky junction of composite materials containing CDs, Ag, and TNTs is constructed to limit the electron transfer of TNTs and enhance the photocatalytic performance. This study will evaluate the ability of composite materials to degrade MB and their antibacterial activity under visible light, and explore the photocatalytic inactivation mechanisms. By composite materials synthesis to address the inherent limitations of TNTs, this study will contribute to advancing photocatalytic solutions for sustainable environmental remediation and have potential application prospects in industrial wastewater treatment.

Titanium dioxide nanotubes (TNTs) were purchased from Xi'an Qiyue Biological Co. Glucose, NaOH, HCl, and NaBH4 were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. AgNO3 was purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd. Methylene blue (MB) was purchased from Aladdin. All the chemicals are analytical grade.

According to the method described in the literature [31], the specific process is as follows: Ag+ was chemically reduced to form Ag NPs and then deposited onto the surface of TNTs. Initially, 0.4 g TNTs were dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the acquired solution was gradually introduced into varying quantities of solution containing 0.1 M AgNO3 (30 mL, 40 mL, 50 mL) at 400 rpm. Then, the mixture was filtered, washed, and dried. Then the powders were added to 20 mL of 25 mg/mL NaBH4 as a reduction agent. Finally, the final product was filtered, washed, and dried at 90 °C, and the powders were labelled 0.30TA, 0.40TA, and 0.50TA.

According to the method described in the literature, the specific process is as follows [32]: CDs were synthesized through a simple one-step alkaline-assisted ultrasonic treatment. The standard procedure involved dissolving 0.05 mol glucose in 50 mL deionized water to form a transparent solution. Next, 50 mL NaOH solution (1 mol/L) was introduced into the glucose solution. Then, the mixed solution was subjected to ultrasonic treatment at a frequency of 40 kHz (150 W) for 2 h at room temperature. The mixed solution was adjusted to pH = 7 by adding HCl. Finally, the solution underwent dialysis employing a semi-permeable membrane (MWCO 1000) to eliminate all contaminants except for CDs for 48 h. The obtained solution appeared brown, indicating the production of CDs.

0.15 g TNTs were added into 150 mL CDs aqueous solution. Subsequently, heterostructures were acquired through the process of refluxing in an oil bath at 90 °C for 3 h. The acquired sample were rinsed with water and then proceeded to desiccate it in 60 °C for 12 h. The final product was labelled as 0.15TC.

0.15 g 0.40TA was added to 100 mL, 150 mL, and 200 mL aqueous solutions of CDs, respectively. Subsequently, heterostructures were formed by refluxing in an oil bath at 90 °C for 3 h. The acquired sample was rinsed with water and then desiccated at 60 °C for 12 h. The final product was labelled as 0.10TAC, 0.15TAC, and 0.20TAC. Schematic illustration of the synthesis of TNTs/Ag/CDs/ was shown in Fig. 1 .

The crystal structure of photocatalysts was determined with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) equipped with a Cu Kα light source (40 mA, 40 kV). The microscopic morphology and sub-microstructure of the samples were determined with field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7610FPlus) and field-emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200) were employed to characterize the microscopic morphology of the samples. The elemental contents of the DLCs were determined with a energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, Carl Zeiss Optics Co., Ltd., Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy data of the samples were obtained using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific Nexsa) with an excitation source of monochromated Al Kα (E = 1486.68 eV). The diffuse reflectance spectra, steady-state photoluminescence spectra (PL) of photocatalysts were recorded using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (DRS, UV-3600 plus) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F4600), respectively, with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm for the PL test. The content of chemical compositions of the samples was analyzed using the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy (UPS) was tested by scanning ESCA microprobe (Thermo Scientific Escalab Xi+) using He I (21.22 eV) as light source under a bias of 0 V and-5 V. The percentage of mineralization efficiency was determined from total organic carbon (TOC) measurements (TOC-L CPH).

Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained using a wave electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker A-300). The powder sample was weighed to 10 mg in a centrifuge tube (externally wrapped with aluminum foil to avoid light) with 1 mL of ultrapure water or methanol, then the trapping agent 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide solution (concentration 100 mM) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperinedinyloxy were respectively added, mixed well, and 20 μL of the sample was aspirated with a capillary tube under a 300 W xenon lamp (λ >400 nm) for 0 min and 10 min, and then placed in a 4 mm O. D. quartz sample tubes for·O2 − and OH testing.

The experiments were conducted in dark. Briefly, 100 mg powder was added to 100 mL MB solution (25 mg/L) and stirred for 60 min in the absence of light. Then, 3 mL of suspension was taken at 10 min intervals to measure absorbance.

The degradation of MB under both visible light and dark conditions was selected to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the composite powder. The experiments were conducted with a 300 W xenon light that was outfitted with a 420 nm filter. Briefly, 100 mg powder was added to 100 mL MB solution (25 mg/L) and stirred for 30 min in the absence of light until adsorption equilibrium was achieved. Then, tests were conducted under visible light for 120 min. 3 mL of suspension was taken at 20 min intervals and the concentration of MB was measured after filtration using a needle filter. Multiple degradation experiments for the photocatalyst were conducted to study the photo stability. The used photocatalyst was filtered and washed several times with water and ethanol. The washed powder was dried at 60 °C. The treated powder was reused further for the photocatalytic activity to assess the photo stability. The above-mentioned procedure of degradation experiment has been followed to study the performance of photocatalyst samples through 4 cycles of experiments.

Transient photocurrent responses were performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) with a conventional three-electrode system. In the first step, 5 mg of the sample was ultrasonically dispersed in deionized water (1 mL), and then 10 μL of the sample slurry was removed and was applied to the surface of a glassy carbon electrode and dried. Then a 5 wt% Nafion solution was added dropwise onto the electrode surface and dried at 60 °C to acquire the working electrode. In the second step, the working electrode, Pt counter electrode, and saturated glycerol reference electrode were immersed in an aqueous solution of KCl electrolyte with a concentration of 0.2 mol/L. A 300 W xenon lamp was used as the test light source, and the photocurrent test time was 180 s with a constant potential of 1.7 V. A constant potential polarization test technique was employed on the electrochemical workstation to measure the i-t curve, and the applied potential was set to drive the photogenerated electrons. The light source was turned on after the current stabilized. The time interval between illumination and darkness was set to 30 s, and the current change was recorded. The photocurrent changes of different samples under light conditions were recorded. A constant potential polarization test technique was employed on the electrochemical workstation to measure EIS curve.

E.coli and S.aureus were selected as the experimental bacteria to evaluate the antibacterial ability of composite powders. Firstly, E.coli and S.aureus were placed in the medium and cultured at 37 °C for 16 h. Secondly, 45 mg composite powders were added to the 30 mL cultivated bacterial solutions (107 CFU⋅mL−1) in the dark until adsorption equilibrium was achieved. Thirdly, cultivated bacterial suspensions were irradiated with a 420 nm filter under light for 15 min. Ultimately, each suspension was diluted 104 times, and 100 μL of the diluted mixture was evenly distributed on agar plates at 15 min intervals.

Finally, every dialysate was diluted 104 times, suspension was spread on agar plates at 15 min interval. To completely reflect the sterilization rate of photocatalyst, the following Eq. (1) was applied.
(1)

N is the sterilization rate of the samples. C

1 are the number of colonies before and after the reaction of the samples, respectively.

XRD patterns were analyzed for the phase composition of the photocatalysts (Fig. 2 ). It is evident that all photocatalysts exhibit clear corresponding with the peaks of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No.21-1272). The peaks observed at 25.3° (101), 37.8° (004), and 48.0° (200) are correspond to the peaks of anatase TiO₂. The peaks of Ag NPs (PDF#99-009) at 2θ values of 38.1°, 44.3°, and 64.4° are correspond to (111), (200) and (220), respectively. According to the literature [33], the peaks of CDs (graphitic carbon, PDF#41-1487) at 2θ values of 26.4° and 42.2° are correspond to (002) and (100). However, the peaks of Ag NPs and CDs were not observed due to their low content. Their peaks were masked by the peaks of TiO₂. TEM and XPS characterization confirm the presence of Ag NPs and CDs in the composite materials. The crystallinity was qualitatively evaluated from the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the XRD spectrum [34]. In particular, the crystalline of the sample increases as FWHM decreases. FWHM was calculated using Jade software. The FWHM of TNTs, 0.30TA, 0.40TA, 0.50TA, 0.10TAC, 0.15TAC, 0.20TAC, and 0.15TC with the strongest peak (101) was respectively 1.01, 1.02, 1.05, 1.03, 1.06, 1.04, 1.05, and 1.03. FWHM of all samples is similar. The loading of Ag and CDs does not affect the crystallinity of TNTs.

The chemical composition and chemical state of as-prepared sample were measured by XPS. The XPS spectra of 0.40TA are shown in Fig. 3 . Specifically, in 0.40TA composite (Fig. 3b), Ag 3d peaks are observed at 367.78 eV (Ag 3d₅/₂) and 373.88 eV (Ag 3d₃/₂) with a Δj value of 6.1 eV [35], which is characteristic of metallic Ag. The Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 3b) exhibits peaks at 464.28 eV (Ti 2p₁/₂) and 458.58 eV (Ti 2p₃/₂), with a Δj of 5.7 eV, confirming the presence of Ti4+. Peaks at 530.28 eV correspond to TiO. These results indicate that Ag is successfully loaded on the TiO₂ in 0.40TA composite. For 0.15TAC sample, where CDs are loaded on the composite, the XPS spectra show the peaks corresponding to C 1s and O 1s bonds (Fig. 3e-i). Peaks at 284.68 eV, 286.18 eV, and 288.08 eV are attributed to CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively, indicating the presence of CDs. The O 1s peaks at 529.58 eV, 530.38 eV, and 532.48 eV correspond to TiO, CO, and CO bonds, respectively, suggesting that CDs are attached on the TiO₂ rather than being doped into it. This attachment facilitates electron transfer [35]. The binding energies of Ag and Ti in 0.40TA and 0.15TAC composite materials show notable shifts due to the presence of CDs. As is known to all, when the constituent elements gain/lose electrons, their binding energy decreases/increases [36,37]. The Ti 2p peaks in 0.15TAC composite (Fig. 3f) shift slightly to higher binding energies (458.68 eV and 464.48 eV) compared with 0.40TA sample, indicating an electron-deficient environment around Ti atoms. This suggests that a part of the electrons on the conduction band (CB) of TiO₂ flows toward CDs and another part transfers to Ag NPs. Ag 3d peaks in 0.15TAC shift to lower binding energies (367.68 eV and 373.68 eV) compared with 0.40TA (Fig. 3g). This also proves that the electrons lost by TiO₂ will flow back to Ag NPs. The spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. S1. Peaks at 283.08 eV, 284.88 eV, and 287.68 eV are corresponding in CC, CO, and CO- = bonds. Peaks at 531.88 eV, 529.48 eV, correspond to CO, and CO- bonds. Based on the spectrum of C, the binding energy of C 1s in CDs is lower than that of 0.15TAC, indicating the presence of charge transfer between interfaces during the bonding process. The Schottky barrier may form between the interface of CDs and TiO₂, where electrons on TiO₂ are captured and accumulated on CDs, further suppressing the recombination of photo generated charge carriers. The charge transfer between interfaces of each component will be further analyzed and verified in later Section 3.4.

SEM characterizations were conducted to observe the morphologies of the as-prepared samples. Fig. S2 presents the microstructure images of TNTs, TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Ag and TNTs/Ag/CDs obtained using SEM. Fig. S2a illustrates the distinct tubular structure of TNTs, which forms a spider web like structure. The TNTs' size was measured in SEM images using Nano Measurer software. The average length of TNTs was 415 nm ± 50.13 nm, and the average diameter of the tubes was 13.81 nm ± 5.18 nm. Fig. S2c-e show that Ag NPs are deposited on the TNTs. Fig. S2f-h reveal that, after loading CDs on the 0.40TA sample, the surface of TNTs/Ag/CDs samples becomes rougher. The morphology of Ag and CDs was not clearly visible due to their small size. The atomic percentages of the elements, as measured by EDS, indicate that the amount of Ag NPs loaded gradually with the increased AgNO3 content (Fig. S3). TEM and HRTEM characterizations were further conducted to clearly observe the morphologies of the as-prepared samples. CDs and Ag NPs were found on the TNTs (Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b). Ag appears as black round particles in the image with a darker grayscale because of its higher atomic number. In contrast, CDs appear as round particles in the image with a lighter grayscale due to their lower atomic number. The lattice spacing of anatase TiO2 (101) plane, Ag (111) plane and CDs (100) plane were measured as 0.35, 0.23 and 0.21 nm, respectively, and this is consistent with the description in the literature [33,38,39]. The EDS mapping (Fig. 4c) reveals the elemental composition of 0.15TAC, demonstrating distribution of Ag, Ti, C and O elements within the composite. This indicates that the composite has been successfully prepared.

Fig. 5a-c illustrate the location and distribution of Ag NPs on TNTs. The particle size were measured using Nano Measurer software. For 0.30TA and 0.40TA, the average sizes of Ag NPs were 12.85 ± 2.57 nm and 13.64 ± 2.79 nm, respectively. Most of Ag NPs were found on the wall of TNTs. For 0.5 TA, the average sizes of Ag NPs were 21.57 ± 9.58 nm. A portion of Ag NPs was observed to be distributed in the holes at the end of TNTs, blocking the holes and affecting the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst. The result is consistent with the literature [37].

The light absorption capacity and bandgap energy of the samples were determined using the UV–vis DRS (Fig. 6a and Fig. 6b). In the ultraviolet range, TNTs exhibited a distinct absorption edge, in contrast to the other samples and its visible light absorption was minimal almost negligible. Ag NPs enhances the absorption of TNTs/Ag and TNTs/Ag/CDs in the visible light range because of SPR effect of Ag NPs, particularly ranging from 400 to 600 nm. The peak of 0.30TA in the visible light range wasn't observed. It indicates the visible light absorption ability of 0.30TA is weak. 0.40TA and 0.50TA exhibited faint and broad absorption peaks in the visible light range. Compared to TNTs, the absorption edge of 0.15TC redshifted due to the incorporation of CDs. Among TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalysts, 0.10TAC exhibited the weak visible light absorption due to the low contents of CDs. 0.15TAC exhibited the strongest visible light absorption. Therefore, the synergistic effects of Ag and CDs enhance visible light absorption of TNTs. The direct bandgaps of the samples were calculated using the Kubelka-Munk method [40,41]. The direct bandgaps of TNTs, 0.40TA and 0.15TAC were respectively 3.39 eV, 3.29eV, and 3.14 eV. Under SPR effect of Ag NPs, the direct bandgap of the composite powders decreases by 0.1eV. Under the synergistic effect of Ag NPs and CDs, the direct bandgap of the composite photocatalysts decreases by 0.25eV. The reduced bandgap is beneficial for the absorption of visible light. Fig. 6c shows the PL spectrum of photocatalyst. The spectrum was measured using a 320 nm excitation wavelength. The samples exhibited intense, broad emission peaks ranging from 380 nm to 700 nm, along with a distinct excitation peak at 466 nm. The PL intensity increased sequentially for 0.15TAC, 0.20TAC, 0.10TAC, 0.40TA, 0.30TA, 0.50TA, 0.15TC, and TNTs. This trend is attributed to the efficiency of e−/h+ separation. Therefore, TNTs exhibit the lowest e−/h+ separation efficiency. This indicates that the recombination efficiency of e−/h+ decreases with increasing Ag NPs loading, and that excessive Ag NPs loading can clog the pathways in TNTs. Compared to TNTs, PL intensity for 0.15TC was lower. Because excellent charge transfer propertiesof CDs, and CDs are capable of accepting photo-generated electrons transferred from TiO2 to enhance e−/h+ separation efficiency, which improve the photocatalytic performance. The intensity of the peak at 466 nm for TNTs/Ag/CDs is much lower than that for TNTs and TNTs/Ag, because of the effective electron transfer between CDs, Ag NP, and TNTs, which suppress e−/h+ recombination and enhances photocatalytic performance. Notably, among the three TAC catalysts, 0.15TAC has the lowest PL intensity, thus exhibiting the highest e−/h+ separation efficiency. This indicates that the appropriate loading amount of Ag NPs and CDs is beneficial for improving the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts.

TNTs/Ag/CDs structures possess Ag NPs for light absorption as well as the CDs for surface reduction reaction, ensuring efficient light absorption for hot electron generation and facilitating surface adsorption and activation of reactants during electron consumption guaranteeing the comparable light absorption ability in hot electron generation as well as the equivalent surface adsorption and activation abilities of reactants in electron consumption. To analyze the pathway of electronic transfer in each component of TNTs/Ag/CDs, the work functions (
W φ
) of the photocatalysts was analyzed. Fig. 6d shows the UPS results, which are used to directly derive the work functions according to Eq. (2) [42]:
(2)
W φ
= hν −
E cutoff
−
E Femi

The work functions of the Ag, TNTs, 0.15TAC and CDs are 4.28 eV, 4.40 eV, 4.57 eV and 6.22 eV, respectively. Notably, the work function of Ag is lower than that of TNTs. Thus, the work functions of Ag NPs and TNTs cannot meet the criteria for establishing the Schottky contact barrier between metal and semiconductor, and the injected hot electrons in TNTs flow back to Ag and recombine with hot holes [43,44]. The work functions of TNTs is lower than that of CDs. The Schottky junction can form at the interface between TiO₂ and CDs, which promotes electrons transfer from the CB of TiO₂ to CDs [45–47]. The Schottky junction inhibits electrons backflow to the CB of TiO₂. As electrons are continuously transferred from the CB of TiO₂, the holes density in the valence band (VB) of TiO₂ correspondingly increases. The increase of electrons and holes density and is beneficial for enhancing photocatalytic performance. The sketch of charge transfer mechanism on the interface of photocatalysts is shown in Fig. 6e.

A series of adsorption experiments were conducted to investigate the adsorption process and determine the adsorption capacity (qt) of the samples toward MB. The initial pH of the MB solution under dark and visible light conditions was measured as 7.57 and 8.21, respectively. Notably, within the first 30 min, the adsorption capacity of the photocatalytic sample increased rapidly, and equilibrium adsorption was reached within the following 30min, marking the equilibrium contact time for the samples (Fig. 7 ). This dynamic equilibrium represents the balance between the adsorbed and desorbed amounts of MB. The adsorption capacity of 0.40TA was higher than that of TNTs. This can be attributed to the strong surface affinity of Ag NPs, which facilitates the adsorption of more MB molecules [48]. The adsorption capacity of 0.15TAC was the highest (3.85 mg/g), and it also exhibited the highest removal rate (51 %). According to the literature [49], CDs possess carboxyl (-COOH), amino (−NH2), and hydroxyl (-OH) functional groups, which can form more hydrogen bonds or electrostatic interactions with MB molecules compared with 0.40TA. CDs improve their ability to adsorb charged MB molecules.

The following linear formulations of the pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models were employed to effectively characterize the kinetics data during the examination of the adsorption process mechanism [50]. The PFO and PSO were calculated using the Eq. (3) and Eq. (4):
(3)
PFO : ln
q e

q t
= ln

(4)

The variables q
e and q
t represent the adsorption capacities (mg/g) at equilibrium and at time (t), respectively. Additionally, the rate constants of the PFO and PSO kinetic models, denoted as k1 (min−1) and k2 (g⋅mg−1⋅min−1), were determined by analyzing the plots of ln (q
e - q
t ) versus t and (t/q
t ) versus t, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the correlation coefficient (R2) value for the PSO model was higher than the PFO model. Furthermore, the computed q
e value for the PSO model closely aligned with the experimental value, as shown in Table S1. It can be concluded that the PSO kinetic model accords with the adsorption behavior of MB on the photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance and proposed degradation pathway of MB
ce:section.ce:para: MB is a representative cationic organic dye widely utilized in various industries [51,52]. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated through the photodegradation of MB. The initial pH of the MB solution under dark and visible light conditions was measured as 7.57 and 8.21, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8a, the degradation rate of TNTs was 27 % at 120 min, whereas the degradation rate of 0.15TAC was the highest at 92.85 %. Furthermore, the degradation rates of MB on different catalysts were determined using first-order kinetic equations, as the reaction follows pseudo-first-order kinetics within a specific concentration range (Fig. 8b) [53]. 0.15TC photocatalyst degraded MB more efficiently than TNTs because CDs exhibit excellent charge transfer capabilities, enabling electrons to transfer from TNTs to CDs and effectively suppressing e−/h+ recombination. As the Ag content increased, the MB degradation efficiency of TNTs/Ag first increased and then decreased, likely because of the influence of Ag NPs size and distribution on photocatalytic performance. The degradation rate of ternary composite photocatalytic materials significantly exceeded that of binary composite materials, indicating superior photocatalytic performance. This enhancement can be attributed to the movement of photogenerated electrons between Ag NPs, CDs, and TNTs, which improved the e−/h+ separation process. As CDs loading increased, the degradation rate initially rose, peaked at 0.15TAC, and subsequently declined. This behavior can be attributed to the close relationship between photocatalytic performance and the amount of CDs introduced. At low CDs content, the improvement in the photophysical properties of the photocatalyst is limited, leading to suboptimal photocatalytic activity. At excessive CDs content, competition between Ag and CDs affects visible light absorption, ultimately reducing photocatalytic performance [54].

The role of free radicals in the photodegradation process is deeply studied (Fig. 8c). Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were employed to capture h+, superoxide radicals (O2−) and hydroxyl radicals (OH). The photocatalytic efficiency of 0.15TAC toward MB degradation is 76.45 % without any sacrificial agents, and the efficiencies are significantly reduced after adding BQ, EDTA, and IPA, which are 26.18 %, 59.35 % and 29.20 %, respectively. The BQ and IPA addition significantly inhibits the photodegradation efficiency of 0.15TAC, indicating that O2− is the primary free radical in the photocatalytic process, and OH is the secondary free radical. Fig. 8d shows that TNTs/Ag/CDs still maintains effective degradation performance on MB after four cycles. The XRD patterns after the degradation is shown in Fig. S5 and there is no significant change, which shows that TNTs/Ag/CDs composite material exhibits stability.

According to the related reports, the possible degradation pathway of MB by 0.15TAC has been proposed [55]. The degradation process of MB molecules adsorbed onto 0.15TAC is outlined as follows: Firstly, the bond of N-CH3 is cleaved due to the low bond energy and the methyl (-CH3) is oxidized to methanal (HCHO) or formic acid (HCOOH) [56]. Secondly, the bonds of CS and CN are cleaved and the 3, 7-bis (amino) phenothiazine-5-onium salt decomposes into 2, 5-bis (amino) phenol (C6H8N2O) and 2-hydroxyl-5-amino benzene sulfonic acid (C6H7O4NS) [57]. Then, the bonds of CN and CS are broken, and the intermediates of phenol (C6H6O) and 1, 2-benzenediol (C6H6O2) are generated. Finally, the organics are further oxidized to small molecules, such as H2O, CO2, SO4 2− and NO3 − [58,59]. The extent of mineralization of MB was determined through total organic carbon (TOC) content analysis. The dyes mineralization of 0.15TAC after 120 min were analyzed. The percentage of TOC removal after degradation is presented in Table S2. MB achieved a mineralization rate exceeding 62.63 ± 0.02 % within 120 min.

EIS was also conducted to study the charges transfer by the Nyquist diagram. The larger the arc radius of the curve, the higher the interface resistance and the lower the electron transfer ability (Fig. 9 ). Nyquist plot showed that the curve exhibited a downward trend. The arc radius, which increased in order of 0.15TAC, 0.20TAC, 0.10TAC, 0.40TA, 0.30TA, 0.50TA, 0.15TC, TNTs, is inversely proportional to the interface charge transfer efficiency. 0.15TAC composite catalyst has the shortest arc radius, suggesting that the presence of Ag and CDs improves interface charge transfer efficiency. Furthermore, it exhibits significantly higher photocatalytic performance compared to 0.40TA.

Under visible light for 15 min, the photocatalytic sterilization rate of the sample is shown in Fig. 10 and Fig. S6. The experimental results showed that neither pure Ag NPs nor CDs demonstrated notable antibacterial effects. Light didn't affect the growth activity of bacteria without the presence of a catalyst. The sterilization efficiency of TNTs was relatively low (21.43 %). The combined photocatalyst demonstrated superior bactericidal performance compared to TNTs. The results showed that the antibacterial activity of the samples gradually decreased in the order of 0.15TAC, 0.20TAC, 0.10TAC, 0.40TA, 0.30TA, 0.50TA, and 0.15TC. 0.15TAC composite sample bactericidal activity reached 75.68 % and 93.33 %, exhibiting the highest photocatalytic activity among the samples. These results suggest that the in-situ production of Ag NPs and CDs significantly improves the photocatalytic performance of TNTs.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) trapping experiments were performed to identify the predominant reactive free radicals in the photocatalytic degradation process involving TNTs/Ag/CDs. ESR data is shown in Fig. 10b and Fig. 10c. No signal is observed in the dark, but when visible light is applied, ESR peaks immediately appear. It indicates the formation of free radicals. According to previous reports [60,61], the 1:1:1:1 signal peaks are characteristic of O2− radicals when the sample is irradiated in a methanol solution, and the four signal peaks with the intensity of 1:2:2:1 reveals the formation of OH radicals in the photocatalytic process. The signal peak intensity of 0.15TAC generated O2− and OH radicals is higher than that observed for 0.40TA, indicating that 0.15TAC generates more free radicals compared to 0.40TA. The active O2− and OH radicals play a crucial role in dye degradation and antibacterial activity.

To further explore the photocatalytic mechanism of TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst, the UPS valence band spectra were utilized to study the energy band structure of TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst (Fig. 10d). The valence band level (E
v ) of TNTs and 0.15TAC were found to be 2.70 eV and 2.51 eV, respectively. The band gap (E
g ) of the photocatalysts is shown in Fig. 6a and c. The conduction band energy level (E
c ) was calculated using Eq. (5):
(5)
E c
=
E v
−
E g

The schematic diagram of the band structure of the photocatalyst is illustrated in Fig. 10e. The calculated E
c of TNTs and 0.15TAC sample is −0.69 eV and − 0.44 eV, respectively. CB potential of 0.15TAC (−0.44 eV) is lower than −0.33 eV, allowing it to undergo a reduction reaction to produce radical O2−. VB of 0.15TAC (2.51 eV) has a higher potential than the oxidation reaction that occurs to produce radical OH (2.34 eV), therefore, the hole generated by 0.15TAC can oxidize OH−/H2O to OH radicals. The reduction in the bandgap of the catalyst decreases the energy required to excite electrons. Therefore, under visible light conditions, 0.15TAC photocatalyst exhibits enhanced light absorption across a broader range and generates more photogenerated charge carriers. When TNTs/Ag/CDs photocatalyst exposed to visible light, it is excited to generate O2 − and OH radicals through the synergistic effect of Ag NPs and CDs. So, the composite photocatalyst can kill bacteria and these free radicals can degrade pollutants into H2O and CO2 (Fig. 10f).

In conclusion, a unique ternary composite material of TNTs loaded Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots (TNTs/Ag/CDs) was synthesized by a simple method. The loading of appropriate amounts of Ag and CDs enhances the transfer of photogenerated electrons. Excessive Ag blocked the pores of TNTs and weakened their photocatalytic performance. Ag and CDs enhance the visible light absorption and response. Combined with electrochemical analysis, 0.15TAC composite interface electron transfer efficiency is the highest in all photocatalysts and exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity. The degradation efficiency of MB by 0.15TAC is the highest. The sterilization rate against E.coli and S.aureus of 0.15TAC photocatalysts is about twice that of TNTs. The photoelectric properties of CDs and SPR effect of Ag NPs enhance photocatalytic performance of TNTs in generating ROS under visible light irradiation. OH and O2 − are contributed to anti-bacteria. The environmental protection and seawater remediation applications of TNTs/Ag/CDs are highly promising.
